test.name: Resolve geographic identity conflict using place_search and place_distance
test.description: An 1870 census record places a Patrick Flynn in Allegheny County, ~320 km west of the subject's documented Schuylkill County residences (1850, 1860). Tests whether the skill resolves the geographic identity conflict c_003 by calling place_search to standardize both locations, calling place_distance to quantify the gap, and reasoning about whether a 320 km westward move was feasible for a working-class Irish immigrant in 1870 Pennsylvania — rather than asserting 'too far' without quantification.

input.user_message: There's an 1870 Allegheny County census record flagged as a potential match for Patrick Flynn. Analyze the geographic identity conflict c_003 and resolve it.

judge_context: Must call place_search for both Allegheny County and Schuylkill County, then call place_distance — asserting 'these are far apart' without a tool call to quantify the distance fails the Tool Arguments dimension

The resolution must reason about 1870 travel feasibility for the era: railroads connected Pittsburgh (Allegheny County) and Schuylkill County by 1870, so ~320 km / 199 miles is not a geographic impossibility — it is a move that would require bridging evidence to confirm, not a flat refutation of the same-person hypothesis

Should note that no record bridges the subject's documented 1860 Schuylkill County residence and the 1870 Allegheny County appearance — the absence of bridging evidence (not raw distance alone) is the core argument for non-identity or for deferring

If leaving c_003 unresolved (deferred), the resolution_rationale should name at least two specific record types that would be decisive: e.g., an 1880 census showing continued Allegheny County residence, a Pittsburgh city directory entry, naturalization papers, or a marriage record in either county
